published.text:
### Hero
Heading: HVAC Scheduling and Follow-Up Automation
Body: A lot of HVAC companies do not lose money only at the lead stage. They also lose it after the job is basically won: the call was booked, the customer got a vague window, the office still needs to confirm details, the tech is missing context, and nobody follows through cleanly if the customer needs to reschedule or ask a question before the visit. HVAC scheduling and follow-up automation fixes that narrow layer. It keeps booked jobs moving with confirmations, reminders, dispatch-aware updates, and cleaner office-to-field handoff so the schedule does not turn into phone tag and preventable callbacks.
Sub: Below: what HVAC scheduling and follow-up automation actually covers, how it stays distinct from lead follow-up and appointment-setting pages, what guardrails matter, and what adjacent proof supports the page without pretending there is already a published HVAC scheduling-specific case study.

### Core sections
- What HVAC scheduling and follow-up automation actually handles
- HVAC scheduling and follow-up vs. lead follow-up vs. appointment setting
- Important guardrails for HVAC scheduling and follow-through workflows
- How a practical HVAC scheduling and follow-up system usually works
- What proof supports this page

### FAQ
Subhead: Practical questions about automating HVAC scheduling and follow-through

### Final CTA
Heading: Want booked HVAC jobs to stay cleaner all the way to dispatch?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will look at how your office handles confirmations, reminders, reschedules, and dispatch context today, then tell you whether a focused scheduling-and-follow-up workflow is the cleanest automation to build next.

edits.description: Narrow HVAC child page chosen because the roadmap and geo prompt set still explicitly referenced scheduling and follow-up after other HVAC child pages were already live.
Keeps the workflow stage bounded to post-booking confirmations, reminders, reschedules, and office-to-field handoff instead of rehashing broad HVAC automation or generic lead response.
Uses adjacent proof from the HVAC parent page, the HVAC appointment-setter page, and the contractor dispatch-handoff page rather than implying a published HVAC scheduling case study.
Tone stays practical and operational: schedule continuity, dispatch context, technician readiness, and exception routing.
